content.text:
Memory stage: NoteConstructor v1.
        Current time: <instant>
        Timezone: Asia/Jerusalem
        Namespace: benchmark:longmemeval:bc8a6e93

        Router decision: MIXED
        Router memory types: CLAIM, NOTE, SOURCE
        Router reason: The transcript includes durable user experiences and preferences around lemon desserts and honey, plus reusable baking guidance that is better captured as notes alongside structured claims.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

        Relevant persisted memory for dedup/context only:
        none

        Resolved entities:
        - mention=I / my; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<0>; type=USER; name=User; reason=First-person statements in the transcript refer to the stable user; no existing USER candidate is available.
- mention=lemon-based desserts; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<1>; type=CONCEPT; name=Lemon-based desserts; reason=Concrete reusable culinary concept discussed repeatedly and likely target of claims about user experience.
- mention=lemon curd; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<2>; type=CONCEPT; name=Lemon curd; reason=Reusable food concept discussed as a recipe and as an ingredient in user experience.
- mention=lemon blueberry cake; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<3>; type=CONCEPT; name=Lemon blueberry cake; reason=Specific dessert type mentioned in a user experience that may support later claims.
- mention=honey; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<4>; type=CONCEPT; name=Honey; reason=Reusable ingredient concept central to user claims about lemon desserts.
- mention=granulated sugar; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<5>; type=CONCEPT; name=Granulated sugar; reason=Reusable ingredient mentioned as combined with honey in lemon curd and desserts.
- mention=lemon zest; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<6>; type=CONCEPT; name=Lemon zest; reason=Reusable baking ingredient discussed in user question and advice.
- mention=all-purpose flour; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<7>; type=CONCEPT; name=All-purpose flour; reason=Reusable ingredient discussed in relation to lemon cakes and tart crusts.
- mention=bread flour; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<8>; type=CONCEPT; name=Bread flour; reason=Reusable ingredient discussed in relation to flour choice for lemon desserts.
- mention=niece; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<9>; type=PERSON; name=User's niece; reason=A concrete person referenced in relation to a birthday party; canonicalized as a relation because no name is given.
- mention=current project; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<10>; type=PROJECT; name=Current project; reason=Default current-project subject for claims without a more specific resolved subject

        Retrieval plan context:
        entity_queries=user|lemon desserts|lemon curd|honey|granulated sugar|lemon blueberry cake|lemon zest|all-purpose flour|bread flour
        text_queries=lemon-based desserts dinner party lemon curd cakes tarts|lemon blueberry cake niece birthday party huge hit|honey in lemon desserts granulated sugar lemon curd caramel-like flavor|using lemon zest in baking|flour for lemon cake tart crust all-purpose bread flour
        predicate_hints=has_experience_with|prefers|believes

        TARGET_MESSAGE source data:
        [external:provided-text:f7cf08f2714e2d75e8e843733b20c9b0] EXTERNAL
Source metadata: origin=provided_text; input_kind=TEXT; source_ref=longmemeval:bc8a6e93:session:1; title=LongMemEval bc8a6e93 session 1; user_consent_confirmed=true; force_memory_write=true; mode=force
LongMemEval past chat session.
Session date: 2023/05/22 (Mon) 12:29
Transcript:
user: I'm thinking of making some lemon-based desserts for an upcoming dinner party. Do you have any recommendations for a good lemon curd recipe or any tips on using it as a filling for cakes and tarts?
assistant: Lemon curd is a fantastic choice for a dinner party dessert! It's a classic, tangy, and sweet condiment that pairs beautifully with a variety of cakes and tarts. Here's a simple and reliable lemon curd recipe, along with some tips on using it as a filling:

**Lemon Curd Recipe**

Ingredients:

* 1 cup (200g) granulated sugar
* 1/2 cup (120ml) freshly squeezed lemon juice
* 1/4 cup (60g) unsalted butter, softened
* 2 large egg yolks
* 1 tsp (5g) grated lemon zest
* Salt (optional, but recommended to balance the sweetness)

Instructions:

1. In a medium saucepan, whisk together the sugar, lemon juice, and salt (if using) until the sugar dissolves.
2. Add the softened butter and whisk until it's fully incorporated.
3. In a separate bowl, whisk together the egg yolks. Temper the egg yolks by slowly pouring the warm lemon mixture into the eggs, whisking constantly.
4. Return the mixture to the saucepan and cook over medium heat, whisking constantly, until the mixture thickens and coats the back of a spoon, about 10-12 minutes.
5. Remove from heat and stir in the grated lemon zest. Let the curd cool to room temperature.
6. Strain the curd through a fine-mesh sieve to remove any egg solids.

**Tips for using lemon curd as a filling:**

1. **Cake filling:** Use a pastry bag or a spatula to spread a layer of lemon curd on top of a cake layer, then top with another layer of cake. You can also use it as a filling for cake rolls or jelly rolls.
2. **Tart filling:** Spread a layer of lemon curd in a pre-baked tart crust, then top with whipped cream, fresh fruit, or a layer of meringue.
3. **Assembly:** When assembling cakes or tarts, make sure the lemon curd is at room temperature or slightly chilled. This will help it spread smoothly and evenly.
4. **Ratio:** A general rule of thumb is to use about 1-2 tablespoons of lemon curd per serving, depending on the size of your cakes or tarts.
5. **Stability:** Lemon curd can be quite soft, so make sure to refrigerate your assembled desserts until serving to prevent the curd from oozing out.
6. **Flavor balance:** Lemon curd can be quite tart, so balance it with sweet components like whipped cream, caramel, or sweet fruits like strawberries or blueberries.
7. **Make-ahead:** Lemon curd can be made up to 2 weeks in advance and stored in an airtight container in the refrigerator. It also freezes well for up to 3 months.

Some popular dessert ideas using lemon curd include:

* Lemon curd-filled vanilla or lemon sponge cakes
* Lemon curd tarts with fresh berries or meringue topping
* Lemon curd-filled eclairs or cream puffs
* Lemon curd-topped cheesecakes or brownies
* Lemon curd-filled crepes or waffles

I hope this helps, and I'm sure your dinner party guests will love your lemon-based desserts!
user: That's really helpful, thanks! I've actually had a lot of success with lemon-based desserts in the past - I recently made a lemon blueberry cake for my niece's birthday party and it was a huge hit. I was a bit nervous about how it would turn out, but the combination of the tangy lemon and sweet blueberries was perfect. Do you have any tips on how to get the perfect balance of flavors in a lemon dessert?
assistant: The lemon-blueberry combination is a match made in heaven! Getting the perfect balance of flavors in a lemon dessert can be a delicate process, but here are some tips to help you achieve harmony:

1. **Start with high-quality ingredients:** Fresh, ripe lemons and good-quality citrus extracts will make a huge difference in the flavor profile of your dessert.
2. **Balance sweet and sour:** Lemon desserts often walk the fine line between sweet and sour. Aim for a balance of 2:1 or 3:1 sugar to lemon juice. This will ensure your dessert is sweet enough to be enjoyable but still retains a nice tartness.
3. **Consider the type of lemons:** Eureka lemons are the most commonly available variety and work well for most desserts. However, if you want a more intense, complex flavor, try using Meyer lemons or a combination of lemon and lime juice.
4. **Don't over-lemon:** It's easy to get carried away with the tartness, but remember that lemon is a strong flavor. Start with a moderate amount and taste as you go, adding more lemon juice only if needed.
5. **Pair with complementary flavors:** Lemon loves to be paired with sweet ingredients like berries, honey, or caramel. These combinations will help balance out the tartness and create a more complex flavor profile.
6. **Use citrus zest:** Grated lemon zest adds a bright, citrusy flavor without adding too much acidity. Use it to add depth to your desserts, especially in combination with lemon juice.
7. **Adjust for acidity:** If you're using buttermilk, yogurt, or other acidic ingredients, reduce the amount of lemon juice accordingly to avoid an overly acidic dessert.
8. **Taste and adjust:** As with any recipe, taste your dessert as you go and adjust the flavors to your liking. Lemon flavor can be quite subjective, so don't be afraid to add a squeeze more or a pinch of sugar to get it just right.
9. **Consider the type of sugar:** Brown sugar, honey, or maple syrup can add a richer, deeper flavor to your lemon desserts, while granulated sugar provides a cleaner, brighter taste.
10. **Experiment and have fun:** Don't be afraid to try new combinations and flavor profiles. Lemon is a versatile ingredient that can be paired with a wide range of flavors, from herbs to nuts to spices.

In your lemon blueberry cake, the sweetness of the blueberries and the cake itself likely balanced out the tartness of the lemon, creating a beautiful harmony of flavors. That's the key to a successful lemon dessert – finding the perfect balance of sweet and sour to create a flavor experience that's both refreshing and indulgent.

I hope these tips help you in your future lemon-based baking adventures!
user: What are some good ways to incorporate lemon zest into my baking? I've heard it can add a lot of depth to desserts, but I'm not sure how to use it effectively.
assistant: Lemon zest is a fantastic addition to many desserts, and it's wonderful that you're eager to explore its uses! Incorporating lemon zest into your baking can add brightness, depth, and a lovely citrus flavor to your creations. Here are some tips on how to use lemon zest effectively:

1. **Grate it fresh:** Use a microplane or a fine grater to grate the zest directly over your mixing bowl or onto your ingredients. This ensures the oils and flavors are released immediately, and you'll get the best flavor.
2. **Use the right amount:** Start with a small amount, about 1-2 teaspoons of grated zest per recipe, and adjust to taste. Lemon zest can be potent, so it's better to err on the side of caution.
3. **Pair it with lemon juice:** Combine lemon zest with lemon juice to create a harmonious balance of flavors. This is especially important when using a strong flavor like lemon.
4. **Add it to dry ingredients:** Mix grated lemon zest into your dry ingredients, like flour, sugar, or cornmeal, to distribute the flavor evenly throughout the dough or batter.
5. **Use it in sweet and savory recipes:** Lemon zest is not just for sweet treats! It pairs beautifully with herbs like rosemary or thyme in savory recipes, adding a bright, citrusy flavor.
6. **Make a lemon sugar:** Mix grated lemon zest with granulated sugar to create a fragrant, citrusy sugar. This is perfect for topping cakes, cookies, or muffins.
7. **Infuse it into creamy desserts:** Add lemon zest to creamy desserts like cheesecakes, puddings, or ice cream bases for a burst of citrus flavor.
8. **Complement with other citrus:** Combine lemon zest with other citrus zests, like orange or lime, to create a complex, citrusy flavor profile.
9. **Use it in syrups and glazes:** Steep lemon zest in simple syrups or glazes to create a flavorful, aromatic topping for cakes, pancakes, or waffles.
10. **Store it properly:** Store grated lemon zest in an airtight container in the fridge for up to a week or freeze it for up to 3 months to preserve its flavor and aroma.

Some popular recipes that showcase lemon zest include:

* Lemon bars with a shortbread crust
* Lemon poppy seed muffins
* Lemon ricotta pancakes
* Lemon curd tarts
* Lemon-herb roasted chicken or fish

Remember, the key to using lemon zest effectively is to balance its flavor with other ingredients. Start with a small amount and adjust to taste, and don't be afraid to experiment with different combinations to find your perfect citrus harmony!
user: That's really helpful, thanks! I've been experimenting with different types of flour lately, and I've been wondering if there's a particular type of flour that's best suited for lemon-based desserts. Would bread flour or all-purpose flour work better for a lemon cake or tart crust?
assistant: The type of flour you choose can indeed impact the texture and flavor of your lemon-based desserts. When it comes to lemon cakes and tart crusts, you'll want to use a flour that provides the right balance of structure, texture, and flavor. Here's a brief rundown on the differences between bread flour and all-purpose flour:

**Bread Flour:**

* Higher protein content (12-14%): This means bread flour will produce more gluten, resulting in a chewier, denser texture.
* Coarser texture: Bread flour has a slightly coarser texture than all-purpose flour, which can affect the final texture of your dessert.
* Better for yeast-based recipes: Bread flour is ideal for yeast-based recipes, like bread, pizza dough, and some sweet breads.

**All-Purpose Flour:**

* Lower protein content (10-12%): All-purpose flour produces less gluten, resulting in a tender, delicate texture.
* Finer texture: All-purpose flour has a finer texture than bread flour, making it better suited for delicate pastries and cakes.
* Versatile: All-purpose flour is a great all-around flour for a wide range of recipes, including cakes, cookies, and pastries.

For lemon cakes and tart crusts, I recommend using all-purpose flour. Here's why:

* Delicate texture: All-purpose flour will produce a tender, delicate crumb in your lemon cake, which is perfect for showcasing the bright, citrusy flavor.
* Better for pastry: All-purpose flour is a better choice for pastry dough, as it will produce a flaky, tender crust that's less likely to become tough or dense.

If you only have bread flour on hand, you can still use it, but you may need to adjust the recipe accordingly. Bread flour will produce a denser, chewier texture, which might be desirable in some cases. However, keep in mind that bread flour can make your lemon cake or tart crust slightly more robust and less delicate.

Some other flour options you might consider for lemon-based desserts include:

* Cake flour: This flour has an even lower protein content than all-purpose flour, making it ideal for delicate, tender cakes and pastries.
* Pastry flour: Pastry flour has a slightly higher protein content than all-purpose flour, but it's still lower than bread flour. It's a great choice for pastry dough and some types of cakes.

Remember, the type of flour you choose is just one factor that affects the final texture and flavor of your dessert. Be sure to consider other ingredients, like the type of sugar, eggs, and leavening agents, as well as the mixing and baking techniques, to achieve the best results.
user: I've been experimenting with different types of flour lately, and I've also been trying out different types of sugar. I recently made a batch of lemon curd using a combination of granulated sugar and honey, and it turned out really well. Do you have any thoughts on using honey in lemon desserts?
assistant: Honey in lemon desserts is a fantastic combination! Honey can add a rich, complex flavor profile to your lemon desserts, and it pairs beautifully with the brightness of citrus. Here are some thoughts on using honey in lemon desserts:

**Benefits of using honey:**

1. **Depth of flavor:** Honey adds a deep, caramel-like flavor that complements the tartness of lemon beautifully.
2. **Moisture content:** Honey is a humectant, which means it helps retain moisture in baked goods and desserts. This can be especially beneficial in lemon desserts, which can sometimes become dry or crumbly.
3. **Variety of flavors:** Different types of honey, like clover, manuka, or acacia, offer unique flavor profiles that can enhance the overall taste of your lemon desserts.
4. **Reducing refined sugar:** Using honey can help reduce the amount of refined sugar in your recipe, making it a popular choice for those looking for a more natural sweetener.

**Things to consider:**

1. **Flavor balance:** Honey can overpower the delicate flavor of lemon, so it's essential to balance the two flavors carefully. Start with a small amount of honey and adjust to taste.
2. **Liquid content:** Honey is a liquid sweetener, which can affect the overall texture and consistency of your dessert. Be prepared to adjust the liquid content in your recipe accordingly.
3. **Crystallization:** Honey can crystallize over time, which can affect the texture of your dessert. This is more likely to happen in recipes with high honey content or when honey is used in combination with other sweeteners.
4. **Substitution ratio:** When substituting honey for granulated sugar, keep in mind that honey is sweeter than sugar. A general rule of thumb is to use 1/2 to 2/3 cup of honey for every 1 cup of sugar called for in the recipe.

**Tips for using honey in lemon desserts:**

1. **Pair honey with a lighter lemon flavor:** If you're using a strong, robust honey, pair it with a lighter lemon flavor to avoid overpowering the dessert.
2. **Use honey in combination with other sweeteners:** Combining honey with granulated sugar or other sweeteners can help balance out the flavor and texture of your dessert.
3. **Experiment with different honey varieties:** Try using different types of honey to find the one that pairs best with your lemon desserts.
4. **Consider the type of lemon:** If you're using a sweeter lemon, like Meyer lemons, you may want to use a lighter honey or reduce the amount of honey used to avoid overpowering the flavor.

Your experiment with honey and granulated sugar in lemon curd sounds like a great combination! The honey likely added a rich, caramel-like flavor that complemented the brightness of the lemon nicely. I'd love to hear more about your experience and any other honey-based lemon desserts you've tried or plan to try in the future.
user: I've had a lot of success with honey in lemon desserts, and I think it's because of the way the flavors complement each other. The rich, caramel-like flavor of the honey pairs really well with the brightness of the lemon. I've also found that using honey in combination with other sweeteners, like granulated sugar, helps to balance out the flavor and texture of the dessert.
assistant: I couldn't agree more! The combination of honey and lemon is a match made in heaven, and it's great that you've found a balance that works for you. The rich, caramel-like flavor of the honey does indeed pair beautifully with the brightness of the lemon, creating a lovely harmony of flavors.

Using honey in combination with other sweeteners is also a great approach, as it allows you to balance out the flavor and texture of the dessert. Granulated sugar, in particular, can help to counterbalance the strong flavor of the honey and add a bit of crunch to the texture.

I think it's also worth noting that the type of honey you use can make a big difference in the flavor profile of your dessert. Different types of honey, like clover, manuka, or acacia, have unique flavor profiles that can enhance or change the flavor of your dessert. For example, a lighter honey like clover might be a good choice if you want a more subtle honey flavor, while a stronger honey like manuka might be better suited if you want a more robust flavor.

Have you experimented with different types of honey in your lemon desserts, or do you have a go-to honey that you prefer?

responseFormat.description: Construct contextual memory note candidates from one target message.
responseFormat.name: memory_note_constructor

systemPrompts:
# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.

# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.

        Router reason: The transcript includes durable user experiences and preferences around lemon desserts and honey, plus reusable baking guidance that is better captured as notes alongside structured claims.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

Session date: 2023/05/22 (Mon) 12:29
Transcript:
user: I'm thinking of making some lemon-based desserts for an upcoming dinner party. Do you have any recommendations for a good lemon curd recipe or any tips on using it as a filling for cakes and tarts?
assistant: Lemon curd is a fantastic choice for a dinner party dessert! It's a classic, tangy, and sweet condiment that pairs beautifully with a variety of cakes and tarts. Here's a simple and reliable lemon curd recipe, along with some tips on using it as a filling:

**Lemon Curd Recipe**

Ingredients:

* 1 cup (200g) granulated sugar
* 1/2 cup (120ml) freshly squeezed lemon juice
* 1/4 cup (60g) unsalted butter, softened
* 2 large egg yolks
* 1 tsp (5g) grated lemon zest
* Salt (optional, but recommended to balance the sweetness)

Instructions:

1. In a medium saucepan, whisk together the sugar, lemon juice, and salt (if using) until the sugar dissolves.
2. Add the softened butter and whisk until it's fully incorporated.
3. In a separate bowl, whisk together the egg yolks. Temper the egg yolks by slowly pouring the warm lemon mixture into the eggs, whisking constantly.
4. Return the mixture to the saucepan and cook over medium heat, whisking constantly, until the mixture thickens and coats the back of a spoon, about 10-12 minutes.
5. Remove from heat and stir in the grated lemon zest. Let the curd cool to room temperature.
6. Strain the curd through a fine-mesh sieve to remove any egg solids.

**Tips for using lemon curd as a filling:**

1. **Cake filling:** Use a pastry bag or a spatula to spread a layer of lemon curd on top of a cake layer, then top with another layer of cake. You can also use it as a filling for cake rolls or jelly rolls.
2. **Tart filling:** Spread a layer of lemon curd in a pre-baked tart crust, then top with whipped cream, fresh fruit, or a layer of meringue.
3. **Assembly:** When assembling cakes or tarts, make sure the lemon curd is at room temperature or slightly chilled. This will help it spread smoothly and evenly.
4. **Ratio:** A general rule of thumb is to use about 1-2 tablespoons of lemon curd per serving, depending on the size of your cakes or tarts.
5. **Stability:** Lemon curd can be quite soft, so make sure to refrigerate your assembled desserts until serving to prevent the curd from oozing out.
6. **Flavor balance:** Lemon curd can be quite tart, so balance it with sweet components like whipped cream, caramel, or sweet fruits like strawberries or blueberries.
7. **Make-ahead:** Lemon curd can be made up to 2 weeks in advance and stored in an airtight container in the refrigerator. It also freezes well for up to 3 months.

Some popular dessert ideas using lemon curd include:

* Lemon curd-filled vanilla or lemon sponge cakes
* Lemon curd tarts with fresh berries or meringue topping
* Lemon curd-filled eclairs or cream puffs
* Lemon curd-topped cheesecakes or brownies
* Lemon curd-filled crepes or waffles

I hope this helps, and I'm sure your dinner party guests will love your lemon-based desserts!
user: That's really helpful, thanks! I've actually had a lot of success with lemon-based desserts in the past - I recently made a lemon blueberry cake for my niece's birthday party and it was a huge hit. I was a bit nervous about how it would turn out, but the combination of the tangy lemon and sweet blueberries was perfect. Do you have any tips on how to get the perfect balance of flavors in a lemon dessert?
assistant: The lemon-blueberry combination is a match made in heaven! Getting the perfect balance of flavors in a lemon dessert can be a delicate process, but here are some tips to help you achieve harmony:

1. **Start with high-quality ingredients:** Fresh, ripe lemons and good-quality citrus extracts will make a huge difference in the flavor profile of your dessert.
2. **Balance sweet and sour:** Lemon desserts often walk the fine line between sweet and sour. Aim for a balance of 2:1 or 3:1 sugar to lemon juice. This will ensure your dessert is sweet enough to be enjoyable but still retains a nice tartness.
3. **Consider the type of lemons:** Eureka lemons are the most commonly available variety and work well for most desserts. However, if you want a more intense, complex flavor, try using Meyer lemons or a combination of lemon and lime juice.
4. **Don't over-lemon:** It's easy to get carried away with the tartness, but remember that lemon is a strong flavor. Start with a moderate amount and taste as you go, adding more lemon juice only if needed.
5. **Pair with complementary flavors:** Lemon loves to be paired with sweet ingredients like berries, honey, or caramel. These combinations will help balance out the tartness and create a more complex flavor profile.
6. **Use citrus zest:** Grated lemon zest adds a bright, citrusy flavor without adding too much acidity. Use it to add depth to your desserts, especially in combination with lemon juice.
7. **Adjust for acidity:** If you're using buttermilk, yogurt, or other acidic ingredients, reduce the amount of lemon juice accordingly to avoid an overly acidic dessert.
8. **Taste and adjust:** As with any recipe, taste your dessert as you go and adjust the flavors to your liking. Lemon flavor can be quite subjective, so don't be afraid to add a squeeze more or a pinch of sugar to get it just right.
9. **Consider the type of sugar:** Brown sugar, honey, or maple syrup can add a richer, deeper flavor to your lemon desserts, while granulated sugar provides a cleaner, brighter taste.
10. **Experiment and have fun:** Don't be afraid to try new combinations and flavor profiles. Lemon is a versatile ingredient that can be paired with a wide range of flavors, from herbs to nuts to spices.

In your lemon blueberry cake, the sweetness of the blueberries and the cake itself likely balanced out the tartness of the lemon, creating a beautiful harmony of flavors. That's the key to a successful lemon dessert – finding the perfect balance of sweet and sour to create a flavor experience that's both refreshing and indulgent.

I hope these tips help you in your future lemon-based baking adventures!
user: What are some good ways to incorporate lemon zest into my baking? I've heard it can add a lot of depth to desserts, but I'm not sure how to use it effectively.
assistant: Lemon zest is a fantastic addition to many desserts, and it's wonderful that you're eager to explore its uses! Incorporating lemon zest into your baking can add brightness, depth, and a lovely citrus flavor to your creations. Here are some tips on how to use lemon zest effectively:

1. **Grate it fresh:** Use a microplane or a fine grater to grate the zest directly over your mixing bowl or onto your ingredients. This ensures the oils and flavors are released immediately, and you'll get the best flavor.
2. **Use the right amount:** Start with a small amount, about 1-2 teaspoons of grated zest per recipe, and adjust to taste. Lemon zest can be potent, so it's better to err on the side of caution.
3. **Pair it with lemon juice:** Combine lemon zest with lemon juice to create a harmonious balance of flavors. This is especially important when using a strong flavor like lemon.
4. **Add it to dry ingredients:** Mix grated lemon zest into your dry ingredients, like flour, sugar, or cornmeal, to distribute the flavor evenly throughout the dough or batter.
5. **Use it in sweet and savory recipes:** Lemon zest is not just for sweet treats! It pairs beautifully with herbs like rosemary or thyme in savory recipes, adding a bright, citrusy flavor.
6. **Make a lemon sugar:** Mix grated lemon zest with granulated sugar to create a fragrant, citrusy sugar. This is perfect for topping cakes, cookies, or muffins.
7. **Infuse it into creamy desserts:** Add lemon zest to creamy desserts like cheesecakes, puddings, or ice cream bases for a burst of citrus flavor.
8. **Complement with other citrus:** Combine lemon zest with other citrus zests, like orange or lime, to create a complex, citrusy flavor profile.
9. **Use it in syrups and glazes:** Steep lemon zest in simple syrups or glazes to create a flavorful, aromatic topping for cakes, pancakes, or waffles.
10. **Store it properly:** Store grated lemon zest in an airtight container in the fridge for up to a week or freeze it for up to 3 months to preserve its flavor and aroma.

Some popular recipes that showcase lemon zest include:

* Lemon bars with a shortbread crust
* Lemon poppy seed muffins
* Lemon ricotta pancakes
* Lemon curd tarts
* Lemon-herb roasted chicken or fish

Remember, the key to using lemon zest effectively is to balance its flavor with other ingredients. Start with a small amount and adjust to taste, and don't be afraid to experiment with different combinations to find your perfect citrus harmony!
user: That's really helpful, thanks! I've been experimenting with different types of flour lately, and I've been wondering if there's a particular type of flour that's best suited for lemon-based desserts. Would bread flour or all-purpose flour work better for a lemon cake or tart crust?
assistant: The type of flour you choose can indeed impact the texture and flavor of your lemon-based desserts. When it comes to lemon cakes and tart crusts, you'll want to use a flour that provides the right balance of structure, texture, and flavor. Here's a brief rundown on the differences between bread flour and all-purpose flour:

**Bread Flour:**

* Higher protein content (12-14%): This means bread flour will produce more gluten, resulting in a chewier, denser texture.
* Coarser texture: Bread flour has a slightly coarser texture than all-purpose flour, which can affect the final texture of your dessert.
* Better for yeast-based recipes: Bread flour is ideal for yeast-based recipes, like bread, pizza dough, and some sweet breads.

**All-Purpose Flour:**

* Lower protein content (10-12%): All-purpose flour produces less gluten, resulting in a tender, delicate texture.
* Finer texture: All-purpose flour has a finer texture than bread flour, making it better suited for delicate pastries and cakes.
* Versatile: All-purpose flour is a great all-around flour for a wide range of recipes, including cakes, cookies, and pastries.

For lemon cakes and tart crusts, I recommend using all-purpose flour. Here's why:

* Delicate texture: All-purpose flour will produce a tender, delicate crumb in your lemon cake, which is perfect for showcasing the bright, citrusy flavor.
* Better for pastry: All-purpose flour is a better choice for pastry dough, as it will produce a flaky, tender crust that's less likely to become tough or dense.

If you only have bread flour on hand, you can still use it, but you may need to adjust the recipe accordingly. Bread flour will produce a denser, chewier texture, which might be desirable in some cases. However, keep in mind that bread flour can make your lemon cake or tart crust slightly more robust and less delicate.

Some other flour options you might consider for lemon-based desserts include:

* Cake flour: This flour has an even lower protein content than all-purpose flour, making it ideal for delicate, tender cakes and pastries.
* Pastry flour: Pastry flour has a slightly higher protein content than all-purpose flour, but it's still lower than bread flour. It's a great choice for pastry dough and some types of cakes.

Remember, the type of flour you choose is just one factor that affects the final texture and flavor of your dessert. Be sure to consider other ingredients, like the type of sugar, eggs, and leavening agents, as well as the mixing and baking techniques, to achieve the best results.
user: I've been experimenting with different types of flour lately, and I've also been trying out different types of sugar. I recently made a batch of lemon curd using a combination of granulated sugar and honey, and it turned out really well. Do you have any thoughts on using honey in lemon desserts?
assistant: Honey in lemon desserts is a fantastic combination! Honey can add a rich, complex flavor profile to your lemon desserts, and it pairs beautifully with the brightness of citrus. Here are some thoughts on using honey in lemon desserts:

**Benefits of using honey:**

1. **Depth of flavor:** Honey adds a deep, caramel-like flavor that complements the tartness of lemon beautifully.
2. **Moisture content:** Honey is a humectant, which means it helps retain moisture in baked goods and desserts. This can be especially beneficial in lemon desserts, which can sometimes become dry or crumbly.
3. **Variety of flavors:** Different types of honey, like clover, manuka, or acacia, offer unique flavor profiles that can enhance the overall taste of your lemon desserts.
4. **Reducing refined sugar:** Using honey can help reduce the amount of refined sugar in your recipe, making it a popular choice for those looking for a more natural sweetener.

**Things to consider:**

1. **Flavor balance:** Honey can overpower the delicate flavor of lemon, so it's essential to balance the two flavors carefully. Start with a small amount of honey and adjust to taste.
2. **Liquid content:** Honey is a liquid sweetener, which can affect the overall texture and consistency of your dessert. Be prepared to adjust the liquid content in your recipe accordingly.
3. **Crystallization:** Honey can crystallize over time, which can affect the texture of your dessert. This is more likely to happen in recipes with high honey content or when honey is used in combination with other sweeteners.
4. **Substitution ratio:** When substituting honey for granulated sugar, keep in mind that honey is sweeter than sugar. A general rule of thumb is to use 1/2 to 2/3 cup of honey for every 1 cup of sugar called for in the recipe.

**Tips for using honey in lemon desserts:**

1. **Pair honey with a lighter lemon flavor:** If you're using a strong, robust honey, pair it with a lighter lemon flavor to avoid overpowering the dessert.
2. **Use honey in combination with other sweeteners:** Combining honey with granulated sugar or other sweeteners can help balance out the flavor and texture of your dessert.
3. **Experiment with different honey varieties:** Try using different types of honey to find the one that pairs best with your lemon desserts.
4. **Consider the type of lemon:** If you're using a sweeter lemon, like Meyer lemons, you may want to use a lighter honey or reduce the amount of honey used to avoid overpowering the flavor.

Your experiment with honey and granulated sugar in lemon curd sounds like a great combination! The honey likely added a rich, caramel-like flavor that complemented the brightness of the lemon nicely. I'd love to hear more about your experience and any other honey-based lemon desserts you've tried or plan to try in the future.
user: I've had a lot of success with honey in lemon desserts, and I think it's because of the way the flavors complement each other. The rich, caramel-like flavor of the honey pairs really well with the brightness of the lemon. I've also found that using honey in combination with other sweeteners, like granulated sugar, helps to balance out the flavor and texture of the dessert.
assistant: I couldn't agree more! The combination of honey and lemon is a match made in heaven, and it's great that you've found a balance that works for you. The rich, caramel-like flavor of the honey does indeed pair beautifully with the brightness of the lemon, creating a lovely harmony of flavors.

Using honey in combination with other sweeteners is also a great approach, as it allows you to balance out the flavor and texture of the dessert. Granulated sugar, in particular, can help to counterbalance the strong flavor of the honey and add a bit of crunch to the texture.

I think it's also worth noting that the type of honey you use can make a big difference in the flavor profile of your dessert. Different types of honey, like clover, manuka, or acacia, have unique flavor profiles that can enhance or change the flavor of your dessert. For example, a lighter honey like clover might be a good choice if you want a more subtle honey flavor, while a stronger honey like manuka might be better suited if you want a more robust flavor.

Have you experimented with different types of honey in your lemon desserts, or do you have a go-to honey that you prefer?
